volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: Amphitryon (Section 2.1.575c - Section 2.2.664)

sections.label: Section 2.1.575c
sections.original: Égone ?
sections.translation: I wish I were so .

sections.label: Section 2.1.575d
sections.original: Tu istic . úbi bibisti ?
sections.translation: You are wishing for that which is fact ; where have you been drinking ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.576
sections.original: Nusquam equidem bibi .
sections.translation: Nowhere , indeed .

sections.label: Section 2.1.576b
sections.original: Quid hoc sit hominis ?
sections.translation: What is this , that is the matter with the fellow ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.576c
sections.original: equidem decies dixi :
sections.translation: Really I have told you ten times over . I am both at home now , I say ( do you mark me ?), and I , Sosia , am with you likewise . Don ’ t I appear , master , to have told you quite distinctly , and quite circumstantially , that this is so .

sections.label: Section 2.1.579b
sections.original: Vah ,
sections.translation: Avaunt ,

sections.label: Section 2.1.580
sections.original: ápage te a me .
sections.translation: get away with you from me .

sections.label: Section 2.1.580b
sections.original: Quíd est negoti ?
sections.translation: What ’ s the matter ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.581
sections.original: Péstis te tenet .
sections.translation: A pestilence possesses you .

sections.label: Section 2.1.581b
sections.original: Nám quor istuc
sections.translation: But why do you say so to me ? I really am quite well and in perfect health , Amphitryon .

sections.label: Section 2.1.583b
sections.original: At te ego faciam
sections.translation: But I ’ ll make you this very day , just as you have deserved , not to be quite so well , and to be miserable instead of your perfect health , if I return home .

sections.label: Section 2.1.585a
sections.original: sequere sis , erum qui ludificas
sections.translation: Follow me , you who in this fashion are making sport of your master with your crack-brained talk ; you , who , since you have neglected to perform what your master ordered , are now come even of your own accord to laugh at your master . Things which neither can happen , and which no one ever yet heard of in talk , you are telling of , you villain ; on your back I ’ ll take care and make those lies to tell this very day .

sections.label: Section 2.1.590
sections.original: Amphitruo , miserrima istaec miseria est servo bono ,
sections.translation: Amphitryon , this is the most wretched of wretchedness to a good servant , who is telling the truth to his master , if that same truth is overpowered by violence .

sections.label: Section 2.1.592
sections.original: Quo id , malum , pacto potest nam ( mecum argumentis puta )
sections.translation: Discuss it with me by proofs . Why , how the plague can such a thing happen , for you now to be both here and at home ? That I want to be told .

sections.label: Section 2.1.594
sections.original: Sum profecto et hic et illíc . hoc cuivis mirari licet ,
sections.translation: I really am both here and there ; this any person has a right to wonder at ; nor , Amphitryon , does this seem more . strange to you than to myself .

sections.label: Section 2.1.596
sections.original: Quo modo ?
sections.translation: In what way ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.596b
sections.original: Nihilo , inquam , mirum magis tibi istuc quam mihi ;
sections.translation: In no degree , I say , is this more strange to you than to myself ; nor , so may the Deities love me , did I at first credit Sosia — me myself , until that Sosia , I myself , made me to believe me myself . In order did he relate everything , as each thing came to pass , when we sojourned with the enemy ;

sections.label: Section 2.1.600
sections.original: edissertavit . tum formam una abstulit cum nomine .
sections.translation: and then besides , he has carried off my figure together with my name . Not even is milk more like to milk than is that I myself like to me myself . For when some time since , before daybreak , you sent me from the harbour home before you —

sections.label: Section 2.1.603
sections.original: Quid igitur ?
sections.translation: What then ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.603b
sections.original: Prius multo ante aedis stabam quam illo adveneram .
sections.translation: I had been standing a long time at the door before I had got there .

sections.label: Section 2.1.604
sections.original: Quas , malum , nugás ? satin tu sanus es ?
sections.translation: Plague on it , what nonsense ! Are you quite in your senses ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.604b
sections.original: Sic sum ut vides .
sections.translation: I ’ m just as you see me .

sections.label: Section 2.1.605
sections.original: Huic homini nescio quid est mali mala obiectum manu ,
sections.translation: Some mischief , I know not what , has befallen this fellow from an evil hand [*] since he left me .

sections.label: Section 2.1.606b
sections.original: Fateor , nam sum obtusus pugnis pessume .
sections.translation: I confess it ; for I have been most shockingly bruised with his fists .

sections.label: Section 2.1.607
sections.original: Quis te verberavit ?
sections.translation: Who has been beating you ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.607b
sections.original: Egomet memet , qui nunc sum domi .
sections.translation: I myself , who am now at home , beat me myself .

sections.label: Section 2.1.608
sections.original: Cave quicquam , nisi quod rogabo te , mihi responderis .
sections.translation: Take you care to say nothing but what I shall ask you . Now , do you answer me . First of all , who this Sosia is , of that I want to be informed .

sections.label: Section 2.1.610
sections.original: Tuos est servos .
sections.translation: He is your servant .

sections.label: Section 2.1.610b
sections.original: Mihi quidem uno te plus etiam est quam volo ,
sections.translation: Really I have even more than I desire by your own one self . Never , too , since I was born , had I a servant Sosia besides yourself .

sections.label: Section 2.1.612
sections.original: At ego nunc , Amphitruo , dico : Sosiam servom tuom
sections.translation: But now , Amphitryon , I say this ; I ’ ll make you , I say , on your arrival , meet with another Sosia at home , a servant of yours , besides myself , a son of Davus , the same father with myself , of figure and age as well

sections.label: Section 2.1.615
sections.original: qua égo sum . quid opust verbis ? geminus Sosia hic factust tibi .
sections.translation: just like myself . What need is there of words ? This Sosia of yours is become twofold .

sections.label: Section 2.1.616
sections.original: Nimia memoras mira . sed vidistin uxorem meam ?
sections.translation: You talk of things extremely wonderful . But did you see my wife ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.617
sections.original: Quin intro ire in aedis numquam licitum est .
sections.translation: Nay , but it was never allowed me to go in-doors into the house .

sections.label: Section 2.1.617b
sections.original: Quis te prohibuit ?
sections.translation: Who hindered you ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.618
sections.original: Sosia ille , quem iam dudum dico , is qui me contudit .
sections.translation: This Sosia , whom I was just now telling of , he who thumped me .

sections.label: Section 2.1.619
sections.original: Quis istic Sosia est ?
sections.translation: Who is this Sosia ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.619b
sections.original: Ego , inquam . quotiens dicendum est tibi ?
sections.translation: Myself , I say ; how often must it be told you ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.620
sections.original: Sed quid ais ? num obdormivisti dudum ?
sections.translation: But how say you ? Have you been sleeping the while ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.620b
sections.original: Nusquam gentium .
sections.translation: Not the slightest in the world .

sections.label: Section 2.1.621
sections.original: Ibi forte istum si vidisses quendam in somnis Sosiam —
sections.translation: Then , perhaps , you might perchance have seen some Sosia in your dreams .

sections.label: Section 2.1.622
sections.original: Non soleo ego somniculose éri imperia persequi .
sections.translation: I am not in the habit of performing the orders of my master in a sleepy fashion . Awake I saw him , awake I now see you , awake I am talking , awake did he , a little while since , thump me about with his fists .

sections.label: Section 2.1.625
sections.original: Quis homo ?
sections.translation: What person did so ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.625b
sections.original: Sosia , inquam , ego ille . quaeso , nonne intellegis ?
sections.translation: Sosia , that I myself ,— he , I say . Prithee , don ’ t you understand ?

sections.label: Section 2.1.626
sections.original: Qui , malum , intellegere quisquam potis est ? ita nugas blatis .
sections.translation: How , the plague , can any one possibly understand ? You are jabbering such nonsense .

sections.label: Section 2.1.627
sections.original: Verum actutum nosces , quom illum nosces servom Sosiam .
sections.translation: But you ’ ll know him shortly .

sections.label: Section 2.1.628
sections.original: Sequere hac igitur me , nam mi istuc primum exquisito est opus .
sections.translation: Follow me this way , then ; for it is necessary for me first to enquire into this . But take care that all the things that I ordered are now brought from the ship .

sections.label: Section 2.1.630
sections.original: Et memor sum et diligens , ut quae imperes compareant ;
sections.translation: I am both mindful and diligent that what you order shall be performed ; together with the wine , I have not drunk up your commands .

sections.label: Section 2.1.632
sections.original: Vtinam di faxint , infecta dicta re eveniant tua .
sections.translation: May the Gods grant , that , in the event , what you have said may prove untrue .

sections.label: Section 2.2.633
sections.original: Satín parva rés est volúptatum in víta atque in aétate agúnda
sections.translation: Is not the proportion of pleasures in life and in passing our existence short in comparison with what is disagreable ? So it is allotted to each man in life ;

sections.label: Section 2.2.635
sections.original: ita dívis est plácitum , volúptatem ut maéror comés consequátur :
sections.translation: so has it pleased the Gods that Sorrow should attend on Pleasure as her companion ; but if aught of good befalls us , more of trouble and of ill forthwith attends us . For this do I now feel by experience at home and in relation to myself , to whom delight has been imparted for a very short time , while I had the opportunity of seeing my husband for but one night ; and now has he suddenly gone away hence from me before the dawn .

sections.label: Section 2.2.640
sections.original: sola híc mihi nunc vídeor , quia ílle hinc abést quem ego amó praeter omnes .
sections.translation: Deserted do I now seem to myself , because he is absent from here , he whom before all I love . More of grief have I felt from the departure of my husband , than of pleasure from his arrival . But this , at least , makes me happy , that he has conquered the foe , and has returned home loaded with glory . Let him be absent , if only with fame acquired

sections.label: Section 2.2.645
sections.original: domúm recipiát se ; feram ét perferam úsque
sections.translation: he betakes himself home . I shall bear and ever endure his absence with mind resolved and steadfast ; if only this reward is granted me , that my husband shall be hailed the conqueror in the warfare , sufficient for myself will I deem it . Valour is the best reward ; valour assuredly surpasses all things :

sections.label: Section 2.2.650
sections.original: libértas salús vita rés et paréntes , patria ét prognáti
sections.translation: liberty , safety , life , property and parents , country too , and children , by it are defended and preserved . Valour comprises everything in itself : all blessings attend him in whose possession is valour .

sections.label: Section 2.2.654
sections.original: Édepol me uxori éxoptatum crédo adventurúm domum ,
sections.translation: By my troth , I do believe that I shall come much wished for by my wife ,

sections.label: Section 2.2.655
sections.original: quae me amat , quam contra amo , praesertim re gesta bene ,
sections.translation: who loves me , and whom , in return , I love : especially , our enterprise crowned with success , the enemy vanquished , whom no one had supposed to be able to be conquered : these , under my conduct and command , at the first meeting , have we vanquished ; but I know for sure that I shall come to her much wished for .

sections.label: Section 2.2.659
sections.original: Quid ? me non rere expectatum amicae venturum meae ?
sections.translation: Well , and don ’ t you think that I shall come much wished for to my mistress ?

sections.label: Section 2.2.660
sections.original: Meus vir hic quidem est .
sections.translation: Surely , this is my husband .

sections.label: Section 2.2.660b
sections.original: Séquere hac tu me .
sections.translation: Do you follow me this way .

sections.label: Section 2.2.660c
sections.original: Nam quid ille revortitur
sections.translation: But why has he returned , when just now he said that he was in haste ? Is he purposely trying me , and is he desirous to make proof of this , how much I regret his departure ? By my faith , against no inclination of mine has he betaken himself home .

sections.label: Section 2.2.664
sections.original: Amphitruo , redire ad navem meliust nos .
sections.translation: Amphitryon , it were better for us to return to the ship .
